title: Chapter 4 — Destroy the landscape
hero.title: Destroy the landscape
hero.lead: Like in Worms: every shot digs a real hole in the scenery. We'll discover the magic trick to “punch a hole” in an image — and you'll get to dig yourself!
s1.lead: Until now, the landscape was a nice fixed background. Now, every ball that lands tears off a piece of terrain. The holes stay: by the end of the round, the battlefield is all pockmarked.
s2.p1: The canvas knows how to draw… but also how to erase in a shape! We switch to destination-out mode: everything we “draw” then becomes an eraser. Draw a circle → a perfect round hole in the terrain.
s2.callout.try: Try it! Click (or drag your finger) on the hill to dig. Look: you can make caves, bridges, overhangs… exactly like in Worms.
s2.sim.title: Dig the terrain!
s2.sim.hint: Tip: aim under a bump to dig a cave and leave a “bridge” above.
s3.h2: Solid or not? The game's rule
s3.p1: How does the game know whether a ball has hit the ground? No need to store a huge image in memory. We keep: the ground height at each spot, and the list of craters. A point is solid if:
s3.callout.tip: The rule. “I'm below the surface of the ground AND I'm in no crater.” Otherwise, it's empty space.
s3.p2: This little rule is enough to create caves and overhangs: a crater dug under a bump leaves the matter above it “floating”.
s3.fig.cap: Below the surface but inside a crater → empty. The bump above still holds.
s3.callout.nerd: For the curious. Testing “am I in a crater?” = comparing the distance to each crater's centre with its radius. That's the Pythagorean theorem! (dx² + dy² ≤ radius²).
s4.h2: The details that make it feel real
s4.li1: 🟫 Scorched rim. We darken the edge of the hole — but only where there is terrain (source-atop mode), otherwise we'd see a circle floating in the sky.
s4.li2: 🌫️ Dust behind the terrain. The impact kicks up dust drawn behind the terrain: the relief hides it, as if it came out of the hole.
s4.li3: 🌱 Scenery carried off. Grass and pebbles are “baked” into the terrain image: the eraser removes them along with the earth.
s5.h2: Well done, you've been all the way round!
s5.lead: You now roughly know how a web game comes to life:
s5.p1: a loop that redraws 60×/s, a bit of maths for the physics and the scenery, a networked referee to play together, and drawing tricks to smash it all up. And above all: we move forward step by step, each one tested before the next.
s5.callout.tip: The pros' secret. Nobody writes a big game in one go. You make a small version that works, you improve it, again and again. Exactly the 4 batches of this notebook.
